stories.title: Fashion Nova Expands L.A. Footprint With 133K-SF Warehouse Deal
stories.excerpt: Fashion Nova is expanding its Southern California logistics side with another industrial lease in Santa Fe Springs. The Los Angeles-based fast-fashion retailer signed a lease to take over the 132,888-square-foot warehouse at 12301 Hawkins Street, according to CoStar. It increases Fashion Nova’s warehouse footprint in Santa Fe Springs by about 33 percent. New York-based real estate investment manager Clarion Partners owns the property in the Heritage Springs Business Park. It includes 12,004 square feet of office space on a 5.4-acre site, according to a JLL marketing brochure. The recently renovated facility features 30-foot clear heights, 18 dock-high doors, two ground-level doors and 175 parking spaces. The owner did not immediately respond to a request for comment. The deal adds to leasing momentum picking up across L.A. County’s industrial market. Leasing activity reached 15.7 million square feet during the second quarter, up 40 percent compared to last year, according to CBRE. The market also posted 2.8 million square feet of positive net absorption — its third consecutive positive quarter after 10 straight quarters of negative absorption. Vacancy declined 22 basis points since the first quarter to 5 percent, although landlords continue to face falling rents. Average asking rents dropped to $1.19 per square foot per month, down 7 percent from a year prior, and down 32.4 percent from the second quarter of 2023. The Mid-Counties submarket, which includes Santa Fe Springs, recorded 1.1 million square feet of positive absorption during the quarter and an average asking rate of $1.23 per square foot. Gregory Cornfield can be reached at gcornfield@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Fashion Nova leases 133K SF warehouse in Santa Fe Springs, expanding footprint 33%
L.A. County industrial leasing reached 15.7M SF in Q2 2026, up 40% YoY
Clarion Partners owns property; market showing strong logistics demand momentum
stories.summary: Fashion Nova signed a 132,888-square-foot warehouse lease in Santa Fe Springs, increasing its local logistics footprint by roughly one-third. The lease reflects broader strength in L.A. County's industrial market, which posted 15.7 million square feet in leasing activity during Q2 2026—a 40% year-over-year jump. The renovated facility at Heritage Springs Business Park features 30-foot clear heights and dock-high doors typical of modern logistics requirements.
stories.impact: Industrial strength in L.A. County signals robust last-mile and e-commerce infrastructure demand, but this is primarily a logistics-sector play with limited direct bearing on multifamily development. However, sustained industrial momentum can drive employment and worker in-migration to nearby areas, strengthening residential demand in adjacent submarkets like Long Beach and inland Riverside/San Bernardino counties over time.

stories.title: Beyond the Flight to Quality: The Office Questions That Aren’t Being Asked
stories.excerpt: Jim Adler The conversation surrounding office real estate often gets stuck on the same-old, same-old: remote work mandates, flight-to-quality trends, and peak interest rates. But beneath those surface headlines, industry leaders are tracking shift-driving dynamics that haven’t yet hit the main stage. Connect CRE reached out to top minds across brokerage, investment, debt placement, and research to ask a simple question: What is the one office question the market isn’t asking right now? From AI infrastructure and workplace friendships to property tax lag and cash-flow valuations, here is what they say should be on your radar. BROKERAGE & ADVISORY Is Your Building AI-Ready? Jim Adler, Executive Vice President, NAI Hiffman Amanda Kross “Much of today’s conversation centers on the rapid advancement of artificial intelligence. But we may be overlooking a more immediate question for commercial real estate: Is my workplace AI-ready? As companies evaluate office space in the years ahead, they may increasingly assess a building’s ‘AI score’ that will reflect two factors. First, whether a building can support a technology-enabled workforce, and second, whether it helps companies attract and retain the talent needed to develop and use AI. For years, office owners have competed by adding better amenities. The next in-demand offering just might be called ‘AI-optimized.'” The Shift in Geographic Footprints Amanda Kross, Executive Managing Director, Financial Services Division, JLL Anna Squires Levine “At a macro level, AI is enabling companies to be increasingly selective about which roles belong in which talent hubs, rather than simply chasing talent to their existing locations. Geopolitical shifts, supply chain realignment, and changing workforce dynamics are quietly causing companies to reassess their geographic footprint. But most of the office conversation hasn’t caught up to that yet.” “Friendship P
bullets: Office market debate overlooks AI workplace readiness, geographic footprint shifts, talent hub selectivity
AI infrastructure and 'AI-ready' building standards emerging as competitive tenant-retention factor
Geopolitical and supply-chain realignment enabling companies to selectively place talent in specific hubs
stories.summary: Industry thought leaders identify three overlooked office-market dynamics: whether buildings can support AI-enabled workforces ('AI-ready' standards), how AI allows companies to selectively place roles in specific talent hubs rather than blanket relocation, and how geopolitical and supply-chain shifts are reshaping corporate geographic footprints. These shifts go beyond the typical remote-work and flight-to-quality narratives dominating headlines.
stories.impact: Little direct relevance to multifamily development in this article, as it focuses on office tenancy and workplace standards. However, if AI-driven selectivity causes certain talent hubs to consolidate (e.g., tech concentrating in Austin, Boston, SF Bay), multifamily developers should monitor which metros gain or lose white-collar employment density, as this will sharpen rent-growth and absorption trajectories in secondary markets.

stories.title: Systima Closes $153M Tax-Exempt CMBS Affordable Housing Deal
stories.excerpt: A private-label securitization closed by Systima Capital Management for a portfolio of nearly 1,300 subsided apartments underscores the potential for use of the commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) market in affordable housing deals. Systima closed the $153 million tax-exempt affordable housing bond deal through the Public Finance Authority securitized by a pool of loans on seven properties by the federal Low-Income Housing Tax Credit (LIHTC) program. The transaction, which utilized tax-exempt CMBS debt for the 1,272-unit portfolio across Wisconsin, Illinois, Florida, Tennessee and Texas were “significantly oversubscribed” with more than $1.2 billion in orders from 19 institutional investors, according to Systima. Ryan Paszczykowski, head of structured investing at Systima, said the tax-exempt CMBS offering in the deal is poised to become more mainstream going forward thanks to strong investor appetite. J.P. Morgan led the transaction as the lead underwriter with Wells Fargo as co-manager, while Systima retained the subordinate Class B certificates. “I think institutional investors are starting to realize the credit protections that currently exist in affordable housing and the significant need for affordable housing,” Paszczykowski told CO. “I think the market is going to grow going forward.” Paszczykowski noted that tax-exempt CMBS financings have been used for affordable housing projects in the past but were typically “one off” that banks executed to free up their balance sheets. He said Systima is seeking to become one of the first dedicated managers to bring this capital markets strategy to the forefront as an avenue to deliver high-risk adjusted returns to investors. The original loan proceeds were used to fund acquisition, construction, rehabilitation and preservation of the affordable housing communities geared toward families earning at or below 60 percent of area median income. The exact locations and names of the individual properties
bullets: Systima closed $153M tax-exempt CMBS affordable housing securitization for 1,272-unit portfolio
Deal across Wisconsin, Illinois, Florida, Tennessee, Texas; $1.2B+ institutional investor orders
Tax-exempt CMBS proving viable financing tool for Low-Income Housing Tax Credit (LIHTC) properties
stories.summary: Systima Capital Management closed a $153 million tax-exempt CMBS securitization backed by 1,272 subsidized apartments across five states. The deal, which includes seven LIHTC-program properties, drew over $1.2 billion in institutional investor orders from 19 investors, signaling strong appetite for tax-exempt CMBS structures in the affordable housing space. The oversubscription suggests this financing approach is gaining mainstream traction.
stories.impact: Tax-exempt CMBS emerges as a viable—and investor-friendly—capital source for affordable housing portfolios, lowering execution risk and refinancing costs for developers with stabilized LIHTC properties. Developers holding or assembling affordable portfolios across secondary markets (Wisconsin, Tennessee, Florida) should consider securitization strategies alongside traditional permanent debt, especially as institutional capital gravitates toward ESG-aligned, yield-generating tax-exempt structures.

stories.title: M&T RCC Provides $141M Bridge Loan to Newly Built Turtle Bay Apartments
stories.excerpt: M&T Realty Capital Corporation (M&T RCC) closed on a $141.4-million bridge loan for Anagram Turtle Bay, a newly constructed 194-unit multifamily community located at 300 E. 50th Stt. in Manhattan’s Turtle Bay neighborhood. The financing was provided to an ownership group led by Global Holdings, MAG Partners, and Safanad. The three-year bridge loan, which includes an extension option, refinanced the property’s construction financing and is designed to support the asset through the final stages of lease-up and stabilization before a planned transition to long-term permanent financing. “Bridge financing plays an increasingly important role in today’s market, particularly for newly delivered, high-quality multifamily assets that are approaching stabilization,” said Joe Pizzutelli, M&T RCC head of national production, “Anagram Turtle Bay represents exactly the type of opportunity where bridge capital can create value by providing flexibility and certainty of execution while the property completes its lease-up and transitions to permanent financing.” The post M&T RCC Provides $141M Bridge Loan to Newly Built Turtle Bay Apartments appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: M&T RCC provides $141M bridge loan to 194-unit Turtle Bay apartment in Manhattan
Loan refinances construction debt, supports lease-up and stabilization pre-permanent financing
Bridge capital enabling newly delivered, high-quality multifamily to de-risk permanent transition
stories.summary: M&T Realty Capital Corporation closed a $141.4 million three-year bridge loan for Anagram Turtle Bay, a 194-unit newly constructed apartment building in Manhattan's Turtle Bay neighborhood. The financing refinanced construction debt and is designed to support lease-up and stabilization before a planned transition to permanent debt. The deal underscores bridge lending's growing role in de-risking the final-stage execution of high-quality multifamily assets.
stories.impact: Bridge lending is becoming a preferred instrument for managing construction-to-permanent transitions in tight liquidity environments, particularly in high-barrier-to-entry markets like Manhattan. Developers completing projects in strong-demand urban cores should budget bridge financing as a strategic tool to buy time for lease-up absorption and lock permanent rates when market conditions improve; this flexibility is especially valuable when permanent financing costs remain elevated relative to stabilized asset yields.

stories.title: Todd Rosenberg’s Pebb Capital Buys South Beach Office Building
stories.excerpt: Todd Rosenberg’s Pebb Capital has purchased the Uno office building in South Beach, as boutique financial firms vie for outposts in Miami. The seven-story building features about 100,000 square feet of leasable space and 106 covered parking spots at 119 Washington Avenue, between First and Second streets, in the upscale South of Fifth neighborhood. While the sale price remains unclear, it’s likely north of $69 million since Acore Capital provided a loan for that amount, property records show. A representative of Pebb Capital has not responded to a request for comment. Property records show an entity named Yantra 119, managed by Longstead at The Corcoran Group’s Cyril Bijaoui, has owned the building since 2011. Bijaoui, who manages and leases the property, declined to comment. During the pandemic, the seller renovated the interiors of the building, which was constructed in 2002. Earlier this month, the entity launched a construction project to add about 10,000 square feet of office space by converting a terrace. The sale comes as South Beach is seeing an uptick in new Class A office buildings, with demand coming from wealthy executives who want to work near their homes. The Fifth, a nearby boutique building backed by Google billionaire Eric Schmidt, is nearing completion after securing a $47.3 million construction loan in 2024. Hedge fund J. Goldman & Company signed a 10,000-square-foot lease, and Sant Ambroeus will open a 7,000-square-foot restaurant on the ground floor. Across the street, the same development team, Sumaida + Khurana and Bizzi + Bilgili, have another luxury office project, dubbed 1100 Fifth Street, in the pipeline. Further north in Sunset Harbour, private equity giant Ares Management opened a 10,500-square-foot office at the five-story Eighteen Sunset development, which was completed in 2024. Boca Raton-based Pebb Capital is making inroads in Miami. In January, the firm purchased a mixed-use building in the Miami Design District for $73 mi
bullets: Pebb Capital acquires 100K-SF Uno office building in South Beach, Miami for ~$69M+
Acore Capital provided $69M loan; seller was converting 10K SF terrace to office
South Beach office seeing renewed activity from financial services firms seeking Miami outposts
stories.summary: Pebb Capital purchased the Uno office building, a 100,000-square-foot, seven-story office asset in South Beach's South of Fifth neighborhood, with Acore Capital providing a $69 million loan. The seller, under Longstead management, had recently renovated the 2002-built property and was planning to add 10,000 square feet via terrace conversion. The deal reflects renewed interest from financial services and other high-value tenants in establishing Miami presences.
stories.impact: Office activity in South Beach is tangential to multifamily development but signals broader South Florida economic vitality and white-collar job growth. However, developers should note that affluent office workers and financial-services talent concentrating in Miami may increase demand for high-end rental housing in nearby submarkets (Brickell, Wynwood, Coconut Grove); monitoring office migration can help multifamily operators time luxury and mixed-use projects in satellite neighborhoods.

stories.title: 1929-Vintage Los Feliz Apartments Sell to Dream Street Capital
stories.excerpt: Marcus & Millichap has completed the sale of 1939 N. Kenmore Ave., an 18-unit multifamily property located in Los Angeles. The property sold for $5.28 million. “Properties with this charm and character that have been owned by the same family for several decades rarely come to market in Los Feliz,” said Jeff Louks, executive managing director investments. “The level of interest throughout the competitive sale process reflects continued demand for well-located multifamily assets. Investor activity remains steady, and we continue to close multiple transactions each month.” Louks and Elliot Sabag, investment specialists in Marcus & Millichap’s Encino office, represented the buyer, Michael Maltzman of Dream Street Capital. The seller was a private seller. Built in 1929, the property totals 18,317 square feet and is situated on a 0.26-acre parcel in Los Feliz. The Spanish-style building had been owned by the same family for several decades before the sale. The buyer plans to complete a renovation and restoration program. The post 1929-Vintage Los Feliz Apartments Sell to Dream Street Capital appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Dream Street Capital acquires 18-unit vintage Los Feliz apartment for $5.28M
1929-built Spanish-style property on 0.26-acre parcel; owned by same family for decades
Buyer plans renovation and restoration; reflects steady investor demand for well-located LA multifamily
stories.summary: Dream Street Capital purchased a 18-unit multifamily property at 1939 N. Kenmore Avenue in Los Feliz for $5.28 million. Built in 1929, the Spanish-style building totaled 18,317 square feet on a 0.26-acre lot and had been held by a single family for several decades before sale. The buyer intends to undertake renovation and restoration, and the deal exemplifies ongoing investor activity in Los Angeles' well-located vintage multifamily stock.
stories.impact: Steady deal flow in Los Feliz—a constrained, walkable, transit-adjacent neighborhood—underscores persistent investor appetite for older, well-positioned LA multifamily assets that support value-add repositioning. Developers focused on LA's emerging neighborhoods should monitor comparable sales in similar codified historic areas (Silver Lake, Atwater Village) to benchmark land and per-unit acquisition costs; older, smaller buildings are becoming redevelopment targets as land constraints tighten.

stories.title: New York People and Company News, Week of July 24, 2026
stories.excerpt: JLL announced that retail real estate veterans Joshua Strauss and Scott Zinovoy have joined the firm’s New York Retail team. Strauss joins as Executive Managing Director and Zinovoy joins as Senior Vice President, and they will work alongside Vice Chairman Patrick A. Smith to expand JLL’s retail leasing capabilities across New York and the country. Cohen & Steers, Inc. appointed Erik Schneberger as Executive Vice President, Chief Marketing Officer. Based in New York, Mr. Schneberger will lead the firm’s global marketing department, overseeing all marketing initiatives across distribution channels and regions worldwide. Schneberger joins Cohen & Steers with over 20 years of marketing experience, building and leading award-winning marketing organizations at some of the industry’s most recognized investment firms. A&G Real Estate Partners, a national real estate advisory firm specializing in lease optimization, lease renegotiations, real estate sales, and strategic real estate solutions, announced that Andrew Jacobs has joined the firm to lead its real estate sales division, effective immediately. Jacobs will report to Emilio Amendola, A&G’s Co-Founder and Co-CEO, and will be based out of the firm’s New York City office. PKF O’Connor Davies, one of the nation’s largest accounting, tax and advisory practices, announced that Anthony DelValle, CPA, has joined the organization as Partner in its Real Estate Tax practice. He is based in the Firm’s New York City office. DelValle brings more than 25 years of experience providing tax planning, compliance and consulting services with a focus on real estate and partnership taxation. DLC announced that Basil Donnelly has been named Chief Financial, Legal, and Business Officer, expanding his executive leadership responsibilities as the company continues its growth as one of the nation’s leading owner-operators of open-air shopping centers. A member of Team DLC since 20
bullets: JLL hired retail veterans Joshua Strauss and Scott Zinovoy to expand New York retail team
Cohen & Steers, A&G Real Estate, PKF O'Connor Davies added senior commercial real estate hires
NYC real estate firms strengthening bench in marketing, leasing, sales, and tax advisory
stories.summary: JLL added Executive Managing Director Joshua Strauss and SVP Scott Zinovoy to its New York retail leasing team. Separately, Cohen & Steers, A&G Real Estate Partners, and PKF O'Connor Davies announced senior hires in marketing, real estate sales, and tax advisory roles, all based in or focused on New York City. The hiring wave reflects confidence in NYC commercial real estate activity.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact on multifamily development. However, elevated commercial real estate recruiting and team-building in NYC signals confidence in the broader CRE market recovery; this often precedes or accompanies gains in residential lending, capital formation, and development activity. Multifamily developers should monitor advisory-firm hiring trends as a leading indicator of transaction velocity and financing availability in key markets.

stories.title: Merritt Picks Up 537K-SF Jax Industrial Park
stories.excerpt: Merritt Properties acquired Center Point Business Park, an 11-building, 537,800-square-foot light industrial park in Jacksonville’s Butler Corridor. The deal is the first major transaction announced since the company secured a $750 million investment led by Centerbridge Partners to accelerate its long-term growth strategy. Merritt Properties acquired Center Point from Plymouth REIT. Royce Rose and Justin Rabin of CBRE National Partners represented the seller in the transaction. The park is 99% leased to 35 tenants representing a diverse mix of industries. The property features 18- to 22-foot clear heights, rear-load configurations, dock-high loading in most suites, concrete tilt-wall construction, 107 dock doors and 1,005 parking spaces. The average tenant occupies approximately 15,100 square feet. Located at 4801-6631 Executive Park Court, the property is approximately 1.2 miles from Interstate 95 and one-half mile from the Florida East Coast Railway, providing last-mile connectivity throughout the market. The post Merritt Picks Up 537K-SF Jax Industrial Park appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Merritt Properties acquires 537.8K-SF Center Point Business Park in Jacksonville for light industrial
11-building park 99% leased to 35 tenants; features 18-22 ft clear heights, 107 dock doors
Merritt backed by $750M Centerbridge Partners investment to accelerate growth
stories.summary: Merritt Properties acquired Center Point Business Park, a 537,800-square-foot, 11-building light industrial park in Jacksonville's Butler Corridor, from Plymouth REIT. The property is 99% leased to 35 tenants with average lease size of 15,100 square feet, and features modern logistics specs (18-22 foot clear heights, 107 dock doors). The deal is Merritt's first major transaction since securing $750 million in growth capital from Centerbridge Partners.
stories.impact: Industrial acquisition activity in Jacksonville indicates strong logistics demand and capital deployment in a secondary market with growing last-mile and e-commerce connectivity (1.2 miles from I-95, 0.5 miles from Florida East Coast Railway). Indirectly, logistics-driven growth in JAX can attract logistics workers and distribution-center employment, supporting downstream residential demand; developers should track industrial absorption and capital flows to predict near-term multifamily demand in secondary logistics hubs like Jacksonville, Columbus, and Inland Empire.

stories.title: AI Firm Legora Takes 98K SF at SL Green’s 11 Madison Avenue
stories.excerpt: Legora, a company that developed a collaborative artificial intelligence-driven workspace for the legal profession, has signed a new 10-year lease for 98,420 square feet — the entire 11th floor — at SL Green Realty’s 11 Madison Avenue. This brings the building to 100 percent occupancy. SL Green was represented by Brian Waterman, Brent Ozarowski and Erik Harris at Newmark. Legora was represented by Justin Haber and Kyle Riker at JLL. The deal seems to represent a new office for Sweden-based Legora, which opened its New York City headquarters in 27,238 square feet at 838 Broadway last year. The asking rent was unclear, but the office asking rent at 11 Madison Avenue was $90 per square foot as recently as March. Legora’s lease brings the total square footage of office leases for SL Green, Manhattan’s largest office landlord, to roughly 1.5 million square feet so far in 2026. The company’s office properties in the borough maintain a pipeline of over 900,000 square feet. “We are excited to welcome another premier tenant to the already impressive tenant roster at 11 Madison Avenue, which includes Sony, UBS, Jim Beam Brands, WME and Pinterest,” said Steven Durels, executive vice president and director of leasing and real property at SL Green. “This new lease is testament to the building’s status as one of the most prominent properties in the exciting Midtown South neighborhood, and further evidence of the incremental demand that AI and technology tenants are bringing to an already strong leasing market.” 11 Madison Avenue serves as a home base for a number of high-level tech tenants, fitting perfectly into the Flatiron District’s tech-friendly area around Madison Square Park. In June 2025, Pinterest signed an 83,000-square-foot lease for 11 years for the building’s entire 13th floor for an asking rent of $90 per square foot. Health technology company Tempus AI signed a lease for 39,565 square feet for 10 years in August of that year, and March 2026 saw the AI-driven sales
bullets: Legora, AI legal-tech firm, leases 98.4K SF (entire 11th floor) at SL Green's 11 Madison Avenue
10-year lease; Legora expanding from 27K SF headquarters at 838 Broadway signed in 2025
Lease reflects AI-sector tenant demand and SL Green's success leasing high-quality Manhattan office
stories.summary: Legora, a Sweden-based AI-driven legal workspace platform, signed a 10-year lease for 98,420 square feet (the entire 11th floor) at SL Green's 11 Madison Avenue in Manhattan. The lease represents a major expansion from Legora's 27,238-square-foot NYC headquarters established at 838 Broadway in 2025. SL Green's asking rent at 11 Madison was $90 per square foot as of March 2026.
stories.impact: Tech and AI tenant demand is stabilizing high-quality Manhattan office, but this does not directly affect multifamily development. However, if AI-sector expansion (Legora, others) continues to attract technical talent to Manhattan and other tech hubs (Austin, SF, Boston), multifamily developers in those markets should expect sustained rental growth and absorption strength; track AI company expansions and HQ moves as forward-looking demand signals for luxury and mid-market apartments near tech clusters.

stories.title: SL Green Reports Improved Leasing Metrics in Q2 2026
stories.excerpt: SL Green is reaping the rewards of a resurgent New York City office market. “It might be the dead of summer, but our team is, of course, hard at work — this is truly when we shine the brightest, are completely dialed in on our business plan, and outwork the marketing,” said CEO Marc Holliday, Thursday afternoon during SL Green’s second-quarter earnings call. Holliday had earlier predicted the firm’s occupancy would rise — and SL Green saw its occupancy rate rise to 94.7 percent for its New York office portfolio, up from 94.4 percent . “Much of what we predicted at our investor conference in December has now played out in ways that improve cash flow,” he said, before listing off the latest shareholder metrics. The firm reported that its funds from operations (FFO) reached $109.6 million, or $1.43 per share, in the second quarter of 2026, compared to the $124.5 million, or $1.63 per share, recorded in the same period last year. The firm’s total revenue increased from $241 million in the second quarter of 2025 to $264 million in the second quarter of 2026, an increase of 9 percent, SL Green reported. The firm reported a net loss of $26.5 million, or 38 cents per share, in the second quarter of 2026, compared to the net loss of $11.1 million, or 16 cents per share, one year ago. The New York-based real estate investment trust (REIT) signed 53 Manhattan office leases in the second quarter of 2026 that totaled 445,161 square feet, with an average rent of $93.17 per square foot and average lease term of 5.8 years, bringing its leasing activity through the first six months of 2026 to 1,374,425 square feet leased in 104 Manhattan office transactions. “A growing scarcity of premier space in desirable Midtown districts has turned the tables in our favor,” said Holliday, who added the firm expects to exceed its internal leasing goals by the end of the year. “The trend continues to move in the right direction.” The flagship deals SL Green inked in the second quarter includ
bullets: SL Green occupancy rises to 94.7% in Manhattan portfolio, up from 94.4% prior quarter
CEO Marc Holliday attributes leasing strength to predicted turnaround; $1.5M SF leased YTD 2026
stories.summary: SL Green reported occupancy improvement to 94.7% for its Manhattan office portfolio in Q2 2026, up from 94.4% sequentially. Funds from operations reached $109.6 million ($1.43 per share), down from $124.5 million ($1.63/share) in Q2 2025 due to FFO per-share dilution, though total revenue increased to $264 million from $241 million. The firm has leased approximately 1.5 million square feet in 2026 and maintains a pipeline of 900,000+ square feet.
stories.impact: Manhattan office stabilization is broadly positive for NYC commercial real estate sentiment but does not directly affect multifamily. However, sustained office occupancy gains and tenant activity in Manhattan often correlate with broader metro hiring, wage growth, and in-migration, which eventually support residential demand in outer boroughs and adjacent submarkets. Developers eyeing Queens, Brooklyn, and Washington Heights should monitor Manhattan office leasing velocity as a proxy for economic strength and tenant-worker retention in the five-borough area.

stories.title: Mandarin Oriental Brickell Key, Live Local high-rise among $420M-plus South Florida construction permits for week ending July 22, 2026
stories.excerpt: 🏆 New Building: The largest permit issued was a $240 million new construction permit for 1 Island Drive in Miami, where Swire Properties is developing The Residences at Mandarin Oriental, Miami and Mandarin Oriental Hotel on Brickell Key. The former Mandarin Oriental Hotel towers on the site were demolished via controlled implosion in April. The redevelopment will include condos and a hotel, totaling about 1.35 million square feet. Job Filing Number: BD23028760001B001 📊 New Building: The second-largest permit was $95 million for 114 Northeast 22nd Street in Miami, where New Sense LLC, an affiliate of Argentina-based HA Emprendimientos, is developing […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Swire Properties issues $240M permit for Mandarin Oriental mixed-use redevelopment on Brickell Key
New Sense LLC gets $95M permit for 36-story, 320+ unit Live Local Act mixed-use in Miami
South Florida issued $420M+ in construction permits week ending July 22, 2026; strong pipeline
stories.summary: South Florida issued multiple large construction permits in the week ending July 22, 2026, including a $240 million permit for Swire Properties' Mandarin Oriental mixed-use redevelopment (condos and hotel, ~1.35M SF) on Brickell Key and a $95 million permit for New Sense LLC's 503,000-SF, 36-story, 320+ unit Live Local Act project in Miami (which landed a $111M construction loan from S3 Capital). The combined week saw $420M+ in permits.
stories.impact: South Florida multifamily pipeline is accelerating, with large-format mixed-use projects (Live Local Act densities) dominating permits. Developers should note that the Live Local Act (tax incentives for downtown residential infill) is driving both construction financing and permit velocity in Miami proper; monitor completion timelines and lease-up for these projects, as deliveries in 2027–2028 will create meaningful supply in core Miami markets. Conversely, supply pressure in downtown may favor assets in emerging submarkets (Wynwood, Allapattah, Little Haiti) and suburban ring markets (Deerfield, Coral Springs, Kendall).

stories.title: UrbanStreet targets former Motorola office for residential conversion
stories.excerpt: UrbanStreet Group is planning a residential conversion of an atrium office building that used to house Motorola, as part of Schaumburg’s the District at Veridian development. During an informal presentation with village officials, UrbanStreet managing partner Bob Burk said that converting the six-story, 360,200-square-foot building would be a “poster child” for redevelopment creativity, according to the Chicago Daily Herald. The project also includes the construction of a sister building that would reach as high as four stories, giving space for the complex to have around 300 units. 18 row houses will connect the complex to D.R. Horton’s Northgate at Veridian […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: UrbanStreet converts former Motorola office (360.2K SF) to 300-unit apartment complex in Schaumburg
First phase District at Veridian project underway; $1B total development; $94M construction loan from Bank OZK
First building on track for early 2027 completion; sister office conversion planned alongside sister building
stories.summary: UrbanStreet Group is planning a residential conversion of the former Motorola office building—a six-story, 360,200-square-foot structure—into approximately 300 apartments, paired with a new four-story sister building, as part of Schaumburg's District at Veridian megaproject. The first phase, funded by a $94 million Bank OZK construction loan, is nearing completion by early 2027. The broader 225-acre former Motorola campus redevelopment totals $1 billion, with planned retail, parking, and a 150-key hotel to follow.
stories.impact: Large-scale office-to-residential conversion in Schaumburg (Chicago metro) signals landlord and developer confidence in suburban apartment demand; with the first phase on schedule and additional phases penciled in, this project will add 300+ units to the northwest Chicago submarket by 2027–2028. Developers competing in Chicago's outer suburbs should monitor construction costs and lease-up absorption at District at Veridian; successful execution de-risks suburban infill conversion models and may unlock additional legacy office-to-residential projects in similarly transit-accessible nodes (Oakbrook, Itasca).

stories.title: As Deerfield loosens waterfront development rules, West Palm tightens them
stories.excerpt: Two South Florida coastal cities are moving in opposite directions on waterfront development, as localities grapple with regulating construction on the region’s most valuable real estate.  Deerfield Beach is considering looser guidelines for building on the waterfront, while West Palm Beach has temporarily reined in development.  Deerfield Beach commissioners voted 3-2 Tuesday for charter amendments that would loosen decadeslong restrictions, including eliminating floor area ratio maximums for commercial and multifamily properties between the Intracoastal Waterway and the Atlantic Ocean, the South Florida Business Journal reported. The change also would lift limits on mixed-use developments and projects with accessory use that […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Deerfield Beach votes 3-2 to loosen waterfront development rules; eliminates FAR caps on multifamily/commercial
West Palm Beach tightens waterfront development rules; cities moving in opposite regulatory directions
Deerfield referendum scheduled for Nov. 3, 2026; final vote Aug. 4
stories.summary: Deerfield Beach commissioners voted 3-2 to relax decades-old waterfront development restrictions, including eliminating floor area ratio (FAR) maximums for commercial and multifamily properties between the Intracoastal Waterway and the Atlantic Ocean. Height and lot coverage caps remain. A final commission vote is scheduled for August 4, with a voter referendum on November 3. Simultaneously, West Palm Beach is tightening waterfront development rules, creating opposite regulatory trajectories in the region.
stories.impact: Deerfield Beach's FAR deregulation on waterfront parcels will unlock previously constrained development capacity, particularly for multifamily and mixed-use projects. Developers with land or entitlements in Deerfield's waterfront corridor (Intracoastal-Atlantic area) should prepare to maximize density if the referendum passes; this could attract significant capital to Deerfield's beachfront infill market. Conversely, West Palm Beach's tightening signals supply constraints there, potentially raising land costs and development risk. Monitor referendum timing (Nov. 3) to guide project timing and capital allocation decisions in South Florida coastal markets.

stories.title: Alonzo Mourning, HTG net $55M financing package for affordable housing
stories.excerpt: NBA hall-of-famer Alonzo Mourning and Housing Trust Group scored a $55.4 million financing package for a Naranja affordable housing complex. The deal comes as the duo has racked up financing in recent years for below-market rate rentals in Miami-Dade County.  Mourning, through his AM Affordable Housing, and Coconut Grove-based Housing Trust Group plan the 115-unit Artisan Pointe project at 26115 South Dixie Highway in an unincorporated area of south Miami-Dade County. The four-story complex is under construction with completion expected in January 2028, according to the developers’ news release.  The financing package includes $31.5 million in low-income housing tax credit […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Alonzo Mourning and Housing Trust Group secure $55.4M financing for 115-unit affordable project in Naranja
Deal includes $31.5M LIHTC equity, $16.9M permanent loan (Berkadia), $7M from CRA/TIF; completion Jan. 2028
Artisan Pointe restricted to 30–70% AMI households; rents $766–$2,145 per unit
stories.summary: Alonzo Mourning's AM Affordable Housing and Coconut Grove-based Housing Trust Group closed a $55.4 million financing package for Artisan Pointe, a 115-unit affordable complex under construction in unincorporated south Miami-Dade County (Naranja). The financing includes $31.5 million in LIHTC equity, a $16.9 million Berkadia permanent loan, and $7 million from Naranja Lakes Community Redevelopment Agency (including tax-increment funding). Completion is expected January 2028; units are restricted to households earning 30–70% of area median income.
stories.impact: Miami-Dade County's affordable housing financing ecosystem is robust, with mixed capital sources (LIHTC, CRA/TIF, permanent debt) enabling smaller-scale developments to move forward. Developers pursuing affordable projects in South Florida should note the viability of this financing structure and monitor CRA fund availability in growth corridors (south Dade, Naranja, OPA-Locka). This project adds to the affordable pipeline in Miami-Dade; observe lease-up and stabilization metrics to validate demand for rents below market ($2,277 average South Florida asking rent).

stories.title: Endeavor boosts Austin office pipeline with 5th and Walsh project
stories.excerpt: Endeavor’s 5th and Walsh project in Downtown Austin, which will also house its new headquarters, is finally getting off the ground.  Plans for the new five-story, 198,000-square-foot office mid-rise and retail building were submitted to the city in March 2022, more than four years ago. The development will feature 21,000 square feet of restaurant and retail space on the ground floor and a below-grade parking garage, according to the Austin Business Journal.  The project is expected to be completed in the later months of 2028, and Endeavor will promptly move into the building from its old headquarters at 500 West […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Endeavor's 5th and Walsh Austin office project (198K SF, five-story) achieves 50%+ preleasing without shovels in ground
Endeavor occupying 25K SF; unnamed tenants committed to 85K SF; completion late 2028
Amazon rumored as anchor tenant in Endeavor-owned Dog's Head area; 45-year development agreement under way
stories.summary: Endeavor is advancing its 5th and Walsh project in Downtown Austin—a 198,000-square-foot, five-story office mid-rise with 21,000 square feet of ground-floor retail—with over 50% of the building preleased despite site work not yet beginning. Endeavor will occupy 25,000 square feet; unnamed tenants have committed to 85,000 square feet. Completion is targeted for late 2028. Separately, Amazon is reportedly in talks to anchor Endeavor's 2,614-acre Dog's Head development, which the city approved for annexation and a 45-year agreement.
stories.impact: Austin's office pipeline is drying up (per article), but Endeavor's pre-leasing success and Amazon's rumored Dog's Head advance signal strong corporate interest in the metro. Amazon's potential robotics manufacturing facility would create thousands of jobs and in-migrate high-skilled workers, dramatically boosting residential demand across Austin's submarket. Multifamily developers should prioritize land banking and feasibility in Austin's southeast corridor (near Dog's Head and I-35) and monitor Amazon's deal closure; if confirmed, this becomes one of the largest employment generators in the Southwest and warrants immediate project acceleration in nearby submarkets (Riverside, Mueller, Mueller).

stories.title: Starwood files data center plan as Virginia residents revolt
stories.excerpt: Barry Sternlicht’s Northern Virginia data center drive is forging ahead, even as residents plan to revolt against Starwood Capital Group’s dreams. Starwood filed a permit application for its two-building project at the 34-acre Plaza 500 on the Alexandria town line in Virginia, the Washington Business Journal reported. The first building, Plaza 500 South, is planned for more than 226,000 square feet, according to the pending application. HKS Inc. is the architect for the project. The first phase of construction is expected to carry a $5 million price tag, according to Fairfax County records. In addition to the two data center […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Starwood Capital files data center permit (226K+ SF, Plaza 500 South) in Alexandria, Virginia
Starwood purchased Plaza 500 site for $165M four years ago; proposed substation within 100 feet of homes sparks opposition
Neighborhood residents planning September protest; Fairfax officials rejected Starwood's proposal to relocate to Chantilly site
stories.summary: Barry Sternlicht's Starwood Capital Group filed a permit application for its two-building data center project at the 34-acre Plaza 500 on the Alexandria-Fairfax County line. The first building (Plaza 500 South) is planned for 226,000+ square feet; Starwood purchased the site four years ago for $165 million. The proposed on-site electrical substation is located within 100 feet of homes in the Bren Mar neighborhood, sparking resident opposition. Fairfax County officials rejected Starwood's counteroffer to relocate the project to an alternate Chantilly site (purchased in spring for $166.8 million). Residents plan a protest during the September planning commission review.
stories.impact: Data center development in Northern Virginia faces neighborhood opposition and zoning friction, slowing the permitting timeline and increasing execution risk. However, Starwood's commitment to two sites ($165M and $166.8M purchases) and pursuit of large-scale data center infrastructure signals confidence in NOVA's AI and cloud-computing sector growth. Multifamily developers in nearby submarkets (Herndon, Chantilly, Fairfax City) should track data center buildouts and employment growth; if NOVA becomes a secondary AI/hyperscale hub, residential demand and rents will follow, justifying premium land costs and construction financing in proximate walkable neighborhoods.
